currency.name: US Dollar
currency.budgetNote: Recommended: 10 USD (Zimbabwe uses several currencies, USD is most common for visitors)

mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Zimbabwe on?
mapQuestions.answer: Africa, in the south. Zimbabwe is landlocked, between Zambia, Mozambique, Botswana and South Africa 🌍

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Zimbabwe?
mapQuestions.answer: Harare, in the north-east. Tree-lined avenues, jacaranda blossoms in spring, and a busy market scene.

mapQuestions.question: How many official languages does Zimbabwe have?
mapQuestions.answer: 16! Shona and Ndebele are the most widely spoken, English is used for school and business, but the constitution recognises sixteen languages in total, one of the most of any country.

mapQuestions.question: What is the most famous waterfall in Zimbabwe?
mapQuestions.answer: Victoria Falls, shared with Zambia. It is one of the Seven Natural Wonders of the World, 1.7 km wide and up to 108 metres tall. The local Tonga people call it "Mosi-oa-Tunya", meaning "the smoke that thunders".

mapQuestions.question: Why is Zimbabwe called Zimbabwe?
mapQuestions.answer: After "Great Zimbabwe", a giant medieval stone city in the south of the country. The word "Zimbabwe" comes from the Shona "dzimba dzemabwe", meaning "houses of stone".

foods.name: Sadza
foods.description: Thick white maize porridge eaten with every meal, scooped up with your right hand and dipped in stew or relish. The Zimbabwean staple, almost identical to South African "pap" and Malawian "nsima".

foods.name: Nyama (grilled meat)
foods.description: Grilled beef, chicken, goat or game meat (kudu, impala, eland) over hot coals. Often served with sadza and a tomato-onion relish.

foods.name: Madora (mopane worms)
foods.description: Dried mopane caterpillars (a real Zimbabwean delicacy!), pan-fried with tomato and onion or eaten as a crunchy snack. Yes, really. Try them.

foods.name: Muriwo
foods.description: Stewed leafy greens (collards, kale or pumpkin leaves) with tomato and onion. Eaten alongside sadza at almost every meal.

foods.name: Boerewors
foods.description: A coiled spiced beef sausage shared with South Africa, grilled over coals at every "braai" (BBQ). Often served in a bread roll for "boerie rolls".

foods.name: Maputi
foods.description: Roasted whole maize kernels, like a chewy version of popcorn. Sold in small twists of paper at bus stations and on the street.

scavengerItems.label: The huge plume of spray rising from Victoria Falls (visible from kilometres away!)
The Great Zimbabwe stone ruins (curved walls, no mortar, 800+ years old)
Elephants in Hwange National Park (one of the biggest elephant populations in Africa)
A huge ancient baobab tree (some are over 2,000 years old)
A rock-hyrax ("dassie") sunbathing on a kopje (small fluffy, weirdly related to elephants)
The Zimbabwe Bird (a soapstone sculpture, on the flag, found at Great Zimbabwe)
A traditional Shona soapstone sculpture (Zimbabwe is famous for them)
The Zambezi River (the fourth-longest river in Africa)
"Mosi-oa-Tunya" sign at Vic Falls ("the smoke that thunders" in Shona)
A black or white rhino in Matobo National Park (heavily protected)
San / Bushman rock paintings on a Matobo kopje (thousands of years old)
A leopard on a granite "kopje" (rocky outcrop) at dawn

geoMatches.description: The capital, in the north-east. Tree-lined boulevards, the avenues full of purple jacaranda blossom in October, and a busy mbira-music scene.
One of the seven natural wonders of the world. 1.7 km wide, up to 108 m tall, with so much spray you can see it from a small plane 20 km away.
The medieval stone city in the south, built between 1100 and 1450 AD by the Shona ancestors. The biggest stone ruin in sub-Saharan Africa.
Zimbabwe's biggest national park, with one of the largest elephant populations in Africa. Lions, leopards, painted wild dogs, and incredible birdlife.
A landscape of giant granite "balancing rock" hills, full of ancient Bushman rock paintings and a stronghold of the black rhino.
One of the world's largest man-made lakes, on the Zambezi River. Drowned trees still stick out of the water decades after the dam was built.

senses.smell: Acacia smoke from a "braai" fire, dust on a dirt road after the first rains, jacaranda blossoms in Harare in October, the spray of Victoria Falls fresh on your face, sun-baked grass on the savanna…
senses.hear: Mbira music (a thumb-piano with a haunting sound), Shona greetings ("Mhoro!" "Mazvita!"), the deep boom of Victoria Falls in flood season, lion calls at night in Hwange, the giggle of children in a village…
senses.taste: Smoky grilled game meat, warm sadza scooped through stew, sweet baobab fruit ("monkey bread"), salty crunchy madora, sun-ripe mangoes in summer…
senses.feel: Cool spray on your face at Vic Falls, dry dust on a savanna road, smooth granite of a Matobo kopje, the warm fur of a curious dassie, the chill of a southern African winter night sky full of stars…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: How big is the "smoke that thunders"?
stories.body: Victoria Falls is one of the seven natural wonders of the world. The Zambezi River, calm and wide upstream, suddenly drops 108 metres into a narrow chasm, sending up a column of spray that can be seen from 50 km away on a clear day. The Falls are 1.7 km wide. The local Tonga people called it "Mosi-oa-Tunya" (the smoke that thunders) for thousands of years before the Scottish explorer David Livingstone became the first European to see it in 1855 and renamed it after Queen Victoria. Both names are still used. In high water (March to May) almost 500 million litres of water go over the edge every minute. The spray drenches everyone on the bridge between Zimbabwe and Zambia.

stories.question: Who built the great stone city of Zimbabwe?
stories.body: In the south of the country lie the ruins of "Great Zimbabwe", a city of curved drystone walls, conical towers and royal enclosures, built between about 1100 and 1450 AD. The walls are made entirely of carefully cut granite blocks, fitted without mortar, some up to 11 metres tall. Around 18,000 people lived there at its peak, making it the biggest medieval city in sub-Saharan Africa. It was the capital of a Shona-speaking kingdom that traded gold and ivory all the way to the Indian Ocean coast and beyond, with Chinese pottery and Persian glass found among the ruins. When European colonists arrived in the 1800s, some of them refused to believe Africans could have built it, despite all the evidence. Modern archaeology has confirmed clearly that Great Zimbabwe was built by the Shona ancestors. The country itself is named after it.

stories.question: What is the Zimbabwe Bird?
stories.body: On every Zimbabwean banknote, on the flag, on coins, on government seals, and on the country's passports, you see the same image: a stylised bird with a long curved beak, perched on top of a column. This is the "Zimbabwe Bird". The original carvings (six of them) were found at Great Zimbabwe in the 1800s. They are soapstone sculptures about 33 cm tall, each on top of a 1.6 m column. Nobody knows exactly which real bird they represent: some experts say a fish eagle, others say a bateleur eagle. They are believed to have been carved between 1200 and 1450 AD, during the peak of Great Zimbabwe. Five of the original birds have been returned to Zimbabwe; one is still abroad. They are the national symbol of Zimbabwe.
stories.thinkingQuestion: A whole country is named after one ancient stone city. What would your country be called if it were named after its oldest place?

stories.question: What is Shona stone sculpture?
stories.body: Zimbabwe is home to one of the world's most remarkable modern art movements: Shona stone sculpture. Starting in the 1950s, a generation of Shona sculptors began carving soft soapstone, springstone and serpentine into smooth, organic figures of spirits, animals and human forms. Three names became legendary worldwide: Henry Munyaradzi, Nicholas Mukomberanwa, and Joram Mariga. Their works are now in the British Museum, the Rodin Museum in Paris, and major galleries across the world. In Harare you can walk around the National Gallery sculpture garden full of them, or visit the artists' workshops at Chapungu. Many sculptures are still carved with hand tools, just rasps and chisels, and polished by hand for days.

stories.question: What is mbira music?
stories.body: The mbira is a small wooden board with thin metal "tongues" mounted on top, plucked with the thumbs. It is one of the oldest African musical instruments, with versions found across the continent (kalimba, thumb piano, sanza), but the Zimbabwean Shona mbira is one of the most developed. In Shona tradition the mbira is sacred, used to call ancestral spirits during all-night "bira" ceremonies, where music, prayer, dance and trance all happen together. Modern Zimbabwean musicians like Stella Chiweshe and Thomas Mapfumo have taken mbira music to global stages, mixing it with electric guitars and full bands. The sound is hypnotic, repetitive in the best way, and unmistakably Zimbabwean.

convoQuestions.question: Best food in Zimbabwe so far?
Have you tried saying "mhoro" or "mazvita" to anyone? How did they react?
Falls, ruins, parks, capital, which has felt the most "Zimbabwe"?
Did you brave the madora (mopane worms)? Be honest.
If you could see one Zimbabwean animal up close, which would you pick?
Great Zimbabwe was built without mortar 800 years ago and is still standing. How would you build something that lasts that long?

wordSearch.hint: Zimbabwean foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: African elephant
animals.description: Hwange has one of the biggest elephant populations on the continent, sometimes 50,000+ in dry season around the pumped waterholes.

animals.name: Hippopotamus
animals.description: In huge pods along the Zambezi River above and below Victoria Falls.

animals.name: African fish eagle
animals.description: Its piercing call echoes across the Zambezi and Lake Kariba. The "sound of Africa".

animals.name: Plains zebra
animals.description: Across all the parks. Sometimes mixed with eland (the biggest antelope on Earth).

animals.name: Warthog
animals.description: Trotting across savanna roads with their tails straight up. Especially common in Hwange.

animals.name: Black rhino
animals.description: Matobo National Park is one of the best places in Africa to track black rhino on foot.

animals.name: Leopard
animals.description: On granite kopjes at dawn in Matobo, or up an acacia tree in Hwange.
